entries.notes: alsoKnownAs (color morphs + alternate names): Mexican Walking Fish, Mexican Salamander, Ajolote, Wild-type Axolotl, Leucistic Axolotl, Golden Albino Axolotl, White Albino Axolotl, Melanoid Axolotl, Copper Axolotl, Axanthic Axolotl, Piebald Axolotl, GFP Axolotl, Mosaic Axolotl, Chimera Axolotl. Foundation entry for the entire Ambystoma mexicanum color-morph complex (hybrid consolidation policy — all morphs share identical care). Critical husbandry points: coldwater (14-22°C, infections >25°C), substrate must be sand-only or bare-bottom (gravel impaction is fatal), copper-sensitive, chytrid-vulnerable (avoid co-housing with non-quarantined frogs). Critically Endangered in the wild but captive-bred for the trade. Long-lived (10-15+ years). Care level: intermediate.

alsoKnownAs: ADF, Dwarf Underwater Frog, Hymenochirus curtipes (sister species commonly sold under the same trade name), Hymenochirus feae, Gold African Dwarf Frog (H. curtipes color morph from Aqua Imports). Hybrid consolidation: H. boettgeri and H. curtipes are visually and behaviorally indistinguishable in the trade and share identical care — covered by one entry with the sister species in alsoKnownAs. Critical husbandry: fully aquatic (lungs — needs surface access), tropical (22-26°C), VERY copper-sensitive, will eat anything fitting in its mouth (avoid neon-tetra-size tank-mates), chytrid moderate risk. Beginner-friendly entry to the amphibian hobby. Often mis-sold as the much larger Xenopus laevis.

alsoKnownAs: Common Platanna, Xenopus, Platanna, Lab Frog, Pipid Frog, Albino Clawed Frog (common albino morph). Fully aquatic but BIG (10-13 cm) and aggressive — eats any tank-mate that fits in its mouth including small fish, shrimp, and other frogs. Species-only tank required. Globally invasive — illegal/restricted in many US states (California, Oregon, Hawaii, New Jersey, others); UK requires CITES paperwork for importation. Research MUST include legal-status caveat. Lab frogs released into wild = serious invasive issue.

alsoKnownAs: Tropical Clawed Frog, Silurana tropicalis (older genus), Xenopus tropicalis. Smaller (4-6 cm) tropical congener of Xenopus laevis. Same predatory care concerns at proportionally smaller scale (eats smaller tank-mates). Less common in trade than X. laevis but the most-used research diploid clawed frog. Care parallels laevis but warmer water (24-28°C).

alsoKnownAs: Common Surinam Toad, Star-Fingered Toad, Aparo, Cururú-de-Suriname. Famous for the female's back-brooding strategy — eggs sink into skin pores and froglets emerge from the back. Large (10-15 cm), fully aquatic, predatory toward small tank-mates. Niche specialist species; trade availability is limited. Same Pipa genus contains P. parva, P. carvalhoi, etc. — only P. pipa covered here; smaller congeners could be added if trade availability warrants.

alsoKnownAs: Amphiuma, Congo Eel, Conger Eel, Two-Toed Salamander, Conger Snake. Large (60-115 cm!) fully aquatic eel-like North American salamander. Specialist species — NOT for beginners. Needs a long tank (120 cm+), powerful filtration, secure escape-proof lid (will leave water during drought conditions), and prey-only diet (earthworms, fish, crayfish). Has been known to bite handlers. Aqua Imports stocks them. Native US Southeast — also legally restricted as 'wildlife' in some US states.

alsoKnownAs: Mud Eel, Siren Salamander, Siren intermedia (Lesser Siren — sister species commonly sold under the same trade name and covered by alsoKnownAs per hybrid policy). Massive (50-95 cm) eel-like neotenic North American salamander with external gills and reduced forelimbs (no hind limbs). Fully aquatic. Predatory and reclusive — eats earthworms, fish, snails, crayfish. Like Amphiuma, needs a large tank, secure lid, and specialist keeper. Native US Southeast. Generally considered specialist-only.
